planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings after flowering or plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; moss phlox requires excellent drainage and rots in wet soil. Shear or cut back by one-third after flowering to maintain compact mounded form and prevent the center from dying out — this keeps plants attractive and full for many years.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean to average soil in full sun; moss phlox is one of the most spectacular spring-blooming groundcovers in temperate gardens, native to rocky outcrops, sandy barrens, and thin-soiled slopes of eastern North America and covering itself so completely with flowers in April and May that the foliage is entirely hidden — creating a solid carpet of color, typically in shades of pink, white, lavender, or red-purple depending on cultivar. The plant itself is a dense, needle-leaved, semi-evergreen mat barely 4–6 inches tall that cascades beautifully over walls, slopes, and rock garden edges and provides attractive dark-green foliage texture through the rest of the season. After the spectacular spring bloom, trim it back by one-third to maintain dense, compact growth and prevent the center from opening up. Extremely drought-tolerant and cold-hardy once established, and among the few groundcovers that holds its own on steep, dry slopes where nothing else will establish. The cultivar range is enormous — 'Emerald Blue,' 'Candy Stripe,' 'Fort Hill,' 'Scarlet Flame,' and many others offer colors from pure white through pink to deep rose and lavender-blue.

planting.note: Direct-sow in early spring or fall in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun; narrowleaf ironweed is one of the most garden-appropriate Vernonia species because it stays much smaller than the common tall ironweed and does not require staking. The cultivar 'Iron Butterfly' is the most commonly available selection, with an even more refined, mounded habit.

directions: Plant in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun; narrowleaf ironweed is a compact, fine-textured species from the Ouachita Mountains of Arkansas and Oklahoma that solves the main problem with common ironweed (V. noveboracensis) — it stays under 24–30 inches in height rather than growing to 6+ feet, making it garden-scale and requiring no staking. The foliage is thread-like and very finely textured, giving the plant a grassy, airy texture through summer that is unusual among ironweeds. In August and September it produces the same brilliant, saturated purple-magenta flowers characteristic of the genus — vivid clusters of tiny brush-like heads — that are magnets for monarchs, swallowtails, fritillaries, and native bees during the critical late-season migration window. The selection 'Iron Butterfly' is particularly uniform and compact. Drought-tolerant, heat-tolerant, and adaptable to clay soils, making it an excellent plant for Gulf Coast and mid-South gardens where its late-season color is most welcome.

planting.note: Direct-sow after last frost or plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Texas sage is native to the alkaline limestone soils of the Edwards Plateau and Trans-Pecos and performs best in lean, well-drained, dry conditions. Rich or moist soil produces lush but floppy growth with fewer flowers. It will behave as a short-lived perennial in zone 8–10 and is reliably perennial in zone 9–10.

directions: Plant in lean, very well-drained, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Texas sage (not to be confused with Leucophyllum frutescens, also called 'Texas sage') is a small, slender-stemmed perennial salvia native to the limestone hills and rocky grasslands of central and west Texas, producing airy spikes of clear, bright-blue flowers through spring and again in fall after rains — an extremely drought-tolerant native that blooms in the same heat and dry conditions that defeat most garden plants. The plant is open and wiry in habit, with small, oval, slightly aromatic leaves, and relies on its profusion of vivid blue flowers rather than bold foliage for ornamental effect. Native bee-adapted with the classic two-lipped, tubular salvia flower, it attracts native bees, mason bees, and hummingbirds. Excellent for low-water Texas Hill Country-style gardens, rock gardens, dry slopes, and xeriscape designs in zones 7–10 where its need for excellent drainage and alkaline soil can be met.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings or plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; canyon sage is native to rocky canyon walls and slopes in the Chihuahuan Desert at elevations of 4,000–7,000 feet and requires excellent drainage. In humid Gulf Coast conditions, it benefits from raised or rocky beds with lean, fast-draining soil.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; canyon sage is a compact, shrubby perennial salvia from rocky canyon habitats of the Chihuahuan Desert in west Texas, New Mexico, and northern Mexico, producing a long succession of vivid violet-blue flowers throughout the warm season. It forms a dense, dome-shaped subshrub of small, aromatic, gray-green leaves with persistent woody lower stems and soft herbaceous upper growth. The flowers are intensely blue-violet — among the most saturated blues in any salvia — and are produced in whorled spikes throughout spring, summer, and fall with particular abundance after rains. It is a hummingbird magnet throughout its bloom season. Canyon sage combines extreme drought tolerance with outstanding ornamental quality and a refined, compact habit that fits into almost any garden setting. In humid Gulf Coast climates, excellent drainage in lean soil is critical to long-term success; it performs excellently in raised beds, rock gardens, and containers.

planting.note: Root hardwood cuttings in late winter or plant container-grown plants in moist to dry, well-drained, adaptable soil in full sun to part shade; red-flowering currant is drought-tolerant once established and native to the dry coastal chaparral and mixed forest of the Pacific Northwest. The cultivar 'King Edward VII' produces the deepest crimson flowers; 'White Icicle' is a white-flowered form.

directions: Plant in moist to dry, well-drained, adaptable soil in full sun to part shade; red-flowering currant is a Pacific Northwest native shrub and one of the most spectacular early-spring-blooming shrubs for Pacific Coast gardens, producing pendulous clusters of deep rose-red to carmine-pink flowers in March and April before the leaves emerge. It is the first major nectar source for returning rufous hummingbirds on their northward spring migration, and hummingbirds converge on flowering specimens in large numbers. The timing of its bloom — earliest of any showy ornamental shrub in coastal Pacific Northwest gardens — combined with its value to hummingbirds and other early pollinators makes it ecologically as well as ornamentally outstanding. The flowers are followed by small, blue-black, bloom-covered berries attractive to birds. The maple-like, aromatic leaves are attractive through summer and the shrub develops an open, graceful form. Highly drought-tolerant once established and naturally adapted to the summer-dry Pacific Coast climate. The cultivar range includes colors from white through all shades of pink to deep crimson.
